intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

body.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

draft.title: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

data.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer persistent sidebar for accessing experiment categories over tab switching
facade.axisTargeted: Navigation structure — persistent sidebar vs horizontal tabs
facade.label: Sidebar navigation
facade.acceptImplies: Prioritize left-side category panel; tabs only for secondary filters within detail views
facade.rejectImplies: Explore top-tab or floating navigation; sidebar may compete with dashboard data display

evidence.hypothesis: Users prefer persistent sidebar for accessing experiment categories over tab switching
evidence.implication: Prioritize left-side category panel; tabs only for secondary filters within detail views

facade.hypothesis: Users building health dashboards prefer clean, uncluttered data visualization over dense information density
facade.label: Minimal line charts
facade.acceptImplies: Embrace sparse, line-based graphs with generous whitespace and subtle grid lines
facade.rejectImplies: Users want richer visual encoding; consider heatmaps, stacked areas, or denser metric layouts

facade.hypothesis: Users building health dashboards prefer prominent metrics (weight, steps, glucose) over explanatory text or trend stories
facade.label: Big number, small context
facade.acceptImplies: Dashboard should lead with large, scannable KPIs—numbers dominate the visual hierarchy
facade.rejectImplies: Dashboard should pair metrics with supporting narrative—why the number matters, what changed, what it means

facade.hypothesis: Animated counters that transition smoothly between values (vs instant number updates) feel more alive and trustworthy for health metrics
facade.axisTargeted: Animated transitions vs instant state change
facade.label: Smooth number tween
facade.acceptImplies: User values fluid, continuous feedback; motion conveys data integrity and encourages lingering attention on metrics
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers fast, glanceable readouts; animation feels wasteful or distracting for functional data display

facade.hypothesis: A friendly, chat-like voice (e.g., 'Hey, let's see how you're doing') will feel more motivating than clinical language for health tracking.
facade.label: Conversational tone
facade.acceptImplies: User prefers warmth and encouragement. Dashboard should feel like a supportive coach, not a medical record.
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers clarity and authority. Dashboard should feel clinical, data-focused, and objective—no personality needed.

facade.hypothesis: A calming, organic color palette (earth tones, desaturated pastels) will feel more inviting for long-term health tracking than bright, clinical interfaces
facade.axisTargeted: warm vs cool; organic vs digital
facade.label: Soft, muted colors
facade.acceptImplies: User leans toward intimate, breathing, non-jarring design—likely wants the dashboard to feel like a personal journal or sanctuary rather than a medical tool
facade.rejectImplies: User may prefer clarity and energy over warmth—perhaps values visual pop, contrast, and modern digital aesthetics to keep motivation high

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimal clinical aesthetics vs. warm, human-centered visual design for health data
Whether user wants data-dense dashboard layout vs. progressive disclosure with one metric at a time
axes.poleA: All at once or step by step?

axes.label: Whether user responds to motivational/encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting voice
Whether user wants celebratory, gamified feedback vs. calm, meditative tracking environment
Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights about patterns
Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations that encourage reflection

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer scannable card layouts for viewing multiple experiments over dense list views, reducing cognitive load when comparing status across experiments
facade.axisTargeted: Information density and comparison pattern (cards vs lists)
facade.label: Card grid with scrolling
facade.acceptImplies: Design experiment overview with card-based grid layout; prioritize visual status indicators (badges, progress rings) over text details
facade.rejectImplies: Adopt compact list view with inline actions and expandable detail rows; optimize for vertical scanning and quick text reference
